target_game.name: Push'em all
target_game.core_loop: Swipe to move the character, use an expanding stick to push crowds of enemies off the platform, and reach the end of the level.
target_game.audience_proxy: Casual gamers of all ages looking for simple, satisfying, and quick action-oriented gameplay sessions.
target_game.visual_style: Minimalist 3D with bright, contrasting colors and simple stickman-like figures.

palette.description: Vibrant blue water and green/sandy platforms contrasted sharply by bright red enemy figures.

centroid_hook.visual_action: Goku is depicted charging a blue energy blast against a vibrant, rainbow-colored background with glowing orbs.
centroid_hook.text_overlay: Dragon Ball Z Dokkan Battle PLAY NOW!

top_archetypes.label: Fail · in-game

centroid_hook.summary: A player attempts a difficult level and immediately fails by crashing a bus.
centroid_hook.visual_action: A hand cursor moves a purple bus, then crashes a red bus into a green one.
centroid_hook.text_overlay: WHY THIS LEVEL IS SO HARD, CAN YOU WIN?

top_archetypes.rationale: 1 creatives across 1 unique advertisers, average age 259d (velocity source: sov). Hook representative: "A player attempts a difficult level and immediately fails by crashing a bus."

game_fit_scores.rationale: This is a static IP-driven anime card-battler creative leaning on Dragon Ball Z brand recognition — none of which Push'em All can borrow without trademark risk. The minimalist stickman/physics-push core loop has zero affinity with a charged-energy-blast hero pose, and the neon magenta/yellow palette clashes with the game's clean blue/green/red hypercasual look. Audience overlap is also weak: Dokkan players skew mid-core gacha/RPG, not the broad casual swipe-and-satisfy crowd. Skip — do not adapt this archetype.

Hard pass: this archetype is built around licensed Dragon Ball IP (Goku SSJ3, Dragon Fist), which creates immediate legal/takedown risk for a hypercasual title that has no anime aesthetic, no charged-attack mechanic, and no narrative VO style. The gold/indigo/purple palette and shounen-anime audience also clash with Push'em All's bright minimalist stickman look and broad casual all-ages audience. Only the abstract "satisfying knockout payoff" idea is transferable — rebuild the satisfaction beat around the stick expanding and shoving a red stickman crowd off the platform, and drop the IP entirely.

The "relaxing bus-sort puzzle" hook is fundamentally misaligned with Push'em All's energetic, competitive crowd-pushing action loop — selling calm/relaxation undermines the satisfying-aggression fantasy that drives this game's installs. Visual frictions are significant too: the archetype's purple/orange puzzle-board palette and stylus-on-2D-grid framing clash with the game's vibrant blue/red 3D stickman platforms and there's no character protagonist to anchor the player. Audience overlaps modestly on the casual hypercasual layer, but we'd recommend skipping this archetype rather than retrofitting it; if forced, only the "ASMR satisfaction" emotional beat is transferable — re-shoot with stickmen tumbling off platforms in a satisfying chain.

This archetype is a documentary-style cross-section reveal of real-world landmarks — visually (muted navy/orange/forest palette, architectural illustration) and tonally (slow curiosity pacing) it's the opposite of Push'em All's bright, kinetic stickman physics gameplay. There's no hook bridge to swiping, pushing crowds, or platform falls, so adapting it would require fabricating a contrived "what's beneath the platform" angle that abandons the proven satisfying-push payoff. Audience overlap exists only loosely (broad casual viewers), and the archetype's tiny sample (1 creative, 1 advertiser, 328d old) signals this is a weak, stale signal — do not prioritize testing.

The fail-bait hook style does broadly map to hypercasual audiences, but the specific concept (driving/parking buses into each other) has zero mechanical overlap with Push'em All's swipe-to-push crowd-control loop — recreating it would misrepresent the product and likely tank install-to-play retention. Visually the white/green/red palette is roughly compatible, but the absence of stickman crowds, platform-edge pushing, or the signature blue water means we'd essentially be rebuilding the creative from scratch. Recommend skipping this archetype and instead borrowing only the "WHY THIS LEVEL IS SO HARD, CAN YOU WIN?" fail-bait framing applied to a genuine Push'em All near-miss (player getting shoved off the platform at the last second).

brief.title: Push'em All — "Oddly Satisfying Chain Wipe"
brief.hook_3s: "Ever played something this satisfying?" — finger swipes, stick expands, red stickmen tumble off the platform in a domino chain splash.
scene_flow: 0–3s HOOK: Top-down close-up of the blue/green platform. A finger swipes across the screen as the player's stickman extends a glowing stick — first row of red enemies launches into the water with a satisfying splash.
3–7s ESCALATION: Camera pulls back. Swipe-trail visible. Stick keeps growing ('SIZE UP!' pops). A chain reaction sends 20+ red stickmen tumbling off both edges in slow-mo splashes.
7–11s PAYOFF: Combo counter spikes ×8, ×12, ×20. Coins burst upward. Level progress bar fills toward the boss platform.
11–14s CLIMAX: One final mega-swipe clears the boss arena — the screen briefly empties, water ripples settle, a tiny 'PERFECT' stamp lands.
14–15s CTA: Game logo + 'PLAY FREE' button pulses on the bright blue water background.
brief.visual_direction: Lock to the game's signature minimalist 3D look: vibrant #2a7dfa water, #56a38b sandy-green platforms, and a sea of #e3001b stickman enemies. Camera stays in the authentic ¾ top-down Push'em All angle — no stylus, no 2D puzzle board. Lean into ASMR-grade physics: exaggerated splashes, slow-mo tumbles, crisp swipe trails, and the in-game HUD (combo multiplier, coin counter, progress timeline, SIZE UP banner) visible at all times to read as real gameplay, not a mock.
brief.rationale: Voodoo's current rotation on Push'em All is all wordless gameplay videos and one playable — there's zero VO-led "satisfaction question" hook in market, despite that archetype trending across hypercasual. This brief imports the archetype's relaxing-question opener and ASMR payoff beat, but re-skins it onto authentic crowd-push physics so we keep the aggressive-fantasy install driver. UA: run as a fresh VO-driven variant against the silent gameplay control to test whether a spoken hook lifts IPM on iOS casual audiences.
scenario_prompts: Hero frame, aspect 9:16, authentic in-game Push'em All moment: ¾ top-down view of a #56a38b sandy-green floating platform surrounded by vibrant #2a7dfa water, a single player stickman at center wielding a long horizontal expanding stick sweeping a dense crowd of #e3001b red stickman enemies off both edges mid-tumble with white splash particles, in-game HUD visible (combo multiplier '×18 COMBO' top-right, coin counter top-left, level progress timeline top-center, yellow 'SIZE UP!' banner mid-screen), minimalist 3D with bright contrasting colors and simple stickman-like figures, clean shadows, no stylus, no puzzle grid, on-screen text 'Ever felt THIS satisfied? 😌' bold white sans-serif lower third. [Audio: VO 'Ever played a game this satisfying?' / punchy upbeat trap 110 BPM with airy ASMR layer / whoosh on swipe 0.3s, watery splash cluster 1.5s, coin chime on combo 2.8s]

Storyboard frame, aspect 9:16, authentic Push'em All gameplay: extreme close-up of the expanding stick mid-swipe knocking a chain of #e3001b red stickmen off a #56a38b green platform into #2a7dfa blue water, slow-motion domino tumble with exaggerated splash arcs, swipe trail glow, HUD shows '×20 COMBO' and full coin counter, minimalist 3D with bright contrasting colors and simple stickman-like figures, no character invented beyond the existing player stickman, on-screen text 'SIZE UP!' yellow burst overlay center. [Audio: no VO / energetic competitive trap drop 115 BPM / stick-extend whoosh, rapid bowling-pin tumble SFX, deep splash thump on final enemy]

Storyboard CTA frame, aspect 9:16, authentic Push'em All endgame: cleared #56a38b green boss platform with calm rippling #2a7dfa blue water, level progress timeline filled to 100%, floating coin burst settling, minimalist 3D with bright contrasting colors and simple stickman-like figures, large 'PLAY FREE' button in #e3001b red with white text bottom-center, game logo above, on-screen text 'Your turn 👇'. [Audio: VO 'Your turn — play free' / triumphant competitive sting resolving to soft pad / coin shimmer, UI button pop, water ripple tail]

brief.title: "Why Is This Level So Hard?" — Last-Second Push-Off Fail
brief.hook_3s: A finger swipes a stickman across a narrow blue-water platform — red enemies swarm, the stick extends — then BAM, our hero gets shoved off the edge at the final inch.
scene_flow: 0-3s: Top-down Push'em All level start. Hand cursor swipes the blue stickman forward, expanding stick spinning. Overlay punches in: 'WHY IS THIS LEVEL SO HARD?'
3-7s: Combo multiplier climbs (x3, x5, x7) as red stickmen tumble into the blue water. Coin counter ticks up. 'SIZE UP!' power-up flashes.
7-11s: Final platform — a huge red mob waits at the goal line. Player swipes to push, but a single red enemy clips the hero from behind.
11-14s: Slow-mo: blue stickman wobbles on the edge, arms flailing, then plops into the water. Big red 'FAIL' stamp.
14-15s: Reset to level start with finger tap. Overlay: 'CAN YOU WIN?' → CTA card.
brief.visual_direction: Authentic Push'em All top-down/3rd-person hybrid camera on a narrow sandy-green (#56a38b) platform surrounded by vibrant blue water (#2a7dfa). Bright red (#e3001b) stickman enemy crowds contrast sharply against the hero's blue stickman. Minimalist 3D, clean shadows, no textures beyond flat shaded polygons. Keep the signature HUD: level progress timeline at top, combo multiplier text mid-screen, coin counter top-right, 'SIZE UP!' power-up burst. Camera shake on the final shove, slow-mo wobble on the edge sells the fail.
text_overlays: WHY IS THIS LEVEL SO HARD?
brief.rationale: Voodoo's current rotation on Push'em All is all straight gameplay videos and one playable — zero fail-bait framing, which is a proven hypercasual scroll-stopper. This brief grafts the trending 'WHY IS THIS LEVEL SO HARD, CAN YOU WIN?' fail hook onto a genuine Push'em All near-miss (hero shoved off at the last inch), so we get the engagement lift of fail-bait without misrepresenting the core loop. Ship as a 15s video first; if CTR beats the current Applovin videos by >15%, port the same beats into the existing playable's intro.
scenario_prompts: Top-down 9:16 in-game screenshot of Push'em All: a single blue stickman hero (#2a7dfa) standing at the edge of a narrow sandy-green platform (#56a38b) surrounded by vibrant blue water, expanding push-stick rotating around him, a dense crowd of bright red stickman enemies (#e3001b) charging from the far end. Authentic HUD: level progress timeline at top, 'COMBO x7' text mid-screen in bold white, coin counter top-right. Minimalist 3D with bright, contrasting colors and simple stickman-like figures, flat shading, no textures. Bold overlay text reads 'WHY IS THIS LEVEL SO HARD?' across the upper third. [Audio: VO (anxious male) 'Why is this level so hard?!' / 130bpm punchy competitive trap with rising tension synth / whoosh on stick spin at 0.5s, crowd-stomp loop, combo chime at 2s]

9:16 dramatic slow-motion in-game frame from Push'em All: the blue stickman hero (#2a7dfa) tipping sideways off the edge of a sandy-green platform (#56a38b) into vibrant blue water, one red stickman enemy (#e3001b) frozen mid-shove behind him, scattered red bodies already splashing below. Game HUD intact: level progress bar nearly full at top, 'SIZE UP!' power-up notification fading, coin counter top-right. Minimalist 3D, bright contrasting colors, simple stickman figures, flat shading. Giant red overlay text 'FAIL 💀' stamped across center. Full HUD visible: empty progress timeline, combo counter at zero, coin total top-right. Minimalist 3D, bright contrasting colors, simple stickman-like figures, flat shading, no textures. Bold yellow overlay text 'CAN YOU WIN?' pulses center-screen above a green PLAY FREE button. [Audio: VO (taunting female) 'Can YOU win?' / 140bpm energetic competitive electro with hand-clap / UI tap-pop on cursor, rising whoosh into button-glow chime]

brief.title: What's Beneath The Platform? — Push'em All Cross-Section Reveal
brief.hook_3s: "What's actually under the platform?" — camera slices the bright blue water away to reveal a secret multi-floor bunker of red stickmen stacked below.
scene_flow: 0-3s: Top-down Push'em All level in motion — stickman swipes, pushes a small red crowd off the edge. They fall... but the camera follows them DOWN past the waterline.

3-7s: Cross-section reveal: the platform peels open like an architectural diagram, exposing 4 stacked underground floors PACKED with red stickman enemies waiting their turn — labeled 'FLOOR 1', 'FLOOR 2', 'BOSS LVL'.
7-11s: Cut back to gameplay POV — player grabs 'SIZE UP!' power-up, stick expands huge, sweeps an entire floor's worth of red figures off in one satisfying combo (x12 multiplier pops).
11-14s: Rapid montage of deeper, weirder floors revealed (lava floor, ice floor, giant boss floor) — each push sends crowds tumbling between layers.
14-15s: End card: stickman hero on top of cleared platform, coin counter spinning, CTA button pulses.
brief.visual_direction: Lock to Push'em All's signature minimalist 3D look: vibrant #2a7dfa water, #56a38b sandy-green platforms, and #e3001b enemy stickmen as the only saturated threat color. The cross-section reveal uses a clean architectural slice (think cutaway diorama) but rendered in the SAME flat-shaded stickman style — no documentary realism, no muted navy/orange. Camera language is punchy and kinetic: quick orbit, snap zoom into the cutaway, then back to overhead gameplay cam. Preserve in-game HUD (level timeline, combo text, coin counter, SIZE UP banner) on every gameplay beat so it reads as authentic footage.
brief.rationale: Voodoo's current Push'em All rotation is all straight gameplay capture and playables with zero narrative hook — this brief injects the trending curiosity/cross-section reveal angle (underrepresented in their stack) while staying mechanically honest to the push-crowds-off-platform payoff. The "secret floors of enemies beneath the platform" conceit gives scroll-stoppers a reason to watch past 3s, then hands off to the proven satisfying-push gameplay. Test against the existing playable as a top-funnel video to see if curiosity-led CTR lifts installs vs. pure gameplay creatives.
scenario_prompts: Hero frame, 9:16 vertical aspect ratio, Push'em All mobile game in-game screenshot style: overhead three-quarter view of a sandy-green (#56a38b) floating platform surrounded by vibrant blue water (#2a7dfa), the platform sliced open in a clean architectural cross-section to reveal four stacked underground floors crammed with bright red (#e3001b) stickman enemies waiting in formation, top floor shows the blue-shirted player stickman mid-swipe with extending push-stick, authentic game HUD visible: level progress timeline at top, combo multiplier text, coin counter, bold white overlay text 'What's UNDER the platform?', minimalist 3D flat-shaded style with simple stickman-like figures, bright contrasting colors, no realistic textures, no muted palette. [Audio: VO (curious whisper) 'Wait... what's actually under there?' / energetic competitive trap beat 140bpm building tension / whoosh on camera dive at 1.5s, low bass thud on reveal at 3s.]

[Audio: no VO / punchy upbeat trap drop 145bpm / satisfying swoosh-thwack on stick swing, rapid chime stack on combo count, splash SFX as crowd hits water.]

Storyboard frame, 9:16 vertical aspect ratio, Push'em All cutaway diagram style frame: architectural cross-section of the level showing four themed underground floors beneath the sandy-green (#56a38b) surface platform — floor 1 normal red (#e3001b) stickman crowd, floor 2 ice-blue tinted, floor 3 lava-orange tinted, floor 4 a giant red boss stickman, surrounded by vibrant blue (#2a7dfa) water on the sides, clean labeled floors 'FLOOR 1 / FLOOR 2 / FLOOR 3 / BOSS', bold overlay text 'Wait... there are FLOORS?!', minimalist 3D flat-shaded simple stickman figures, bright contrasting colors, authentic Push'em All asset style not documentary realism.
